cataloging_subject.label: Saint Erkenwald
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous 352-line Middle English alliterative poem conventionally called Saint Erkenwald. It survives uniquely in British Library Harley MS 2250, fols. 72v-75v, and narrates the bishop's miraculous encounter with a just pagan judge found beneath St Paul's Cathedral. CME90053 encodes the whole poem through the OTA and Gollancz textual chain. The subject is not Erkenwald's complete historical biography, the entire Harley manuscript, Gollancz's preface and notes, or the broader works sometimes attributed to the Gawain/Pearl Poet.

catalog_summary.title: Saint Erkenwald
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; probably slightly after 1386 and no later than 1420; sole witness copied in the second half of the fifteenth century
catalog_summary.region: England; sole-witness language localized to Cheshire (eLALME LP 419); London and St Paul's Cathedral are the setting, not a proved composition place

assertion.notes: The expanded modern spelling used by CME90053 and the principal source edition.
DIMEV record 706 identifies this as the manuscript rubric/title.
The shorter Latin form appears as the CME XML work head and in the OTA source description.

titles.value: At London in England not full long sithen

assertion.notes: DIMEV preserves the manuscript reading 'sithen'. CME90053 instead places Gollancz's emended 'tyme' in the line and adds '[MS: sythen]'.

titles.value: And alle the belles in the burghe beryd at ones

assertion.notes: The source spelling of line 352 as encoded in CME90053; DIMEV confirms the same explicit with manuscript characters.

agents.name: Anonymous
notes: The author's identity is unknown. The label does not assert identity with the poet of Pearl, Patience, Cleanness or Sir Gawain and the Green Knight.

agents.name: Gawain/Pearl Poet
notes: An unidentified authorial construct associated with Pearl, Patience, Cleanness and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight; identity with the Saint Erkenwald poet is disputed.

agents.name: Ralph Strode
notes: Gollancz speculatively associated the poem with Chaucer's 'philosophical Strode'; this is attribution history, not accepted authorship.

agents.name: John Massey of Cotton
notes: Peterson's proposed Cheshire authorship candidate, reported by Schustereder; no scholarly consensus is asserted.

assertion.notes: No source identifies a securely known author.
Similarities with Pearl, Patience, Cleanness and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight have prompted attribution, but common authorship remains unresolved and is not the catalog default.
Gollancz says one 'fondly plays with the fancy' that Ralph Strode was concerned and warns that theorizing is dangerous without clear evidence. This historical speculation is not the catalog default.
Schustereder reports Peterson's conclusion that John Massey of Cotton met his proposed authorship criteria, while also stating that the associated authorship discussion had reached no consensus.

scope.description: Editor of the 1922 immediate source edition
Fresh transcript of Harley MS 2250, fols. 72v-75v, underlying the 1922 edition
Editor of the 1881 first known printed edition, a predecessor consulted by Gollancz
Editor and translator of the collateral 2024 current critical edition

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; probably slightly after 1386 and no later than 1420

assertion.notes: Schustereder reports Peterson's view that linguistic evidence points probably slightly later than 1386 and no later than 1420. Only the explicit upper bound is normalized; 'slightly later' is not converted into an invented exact lower year.
Gollancz says the poem supplies no internal date evidence and infers 1386 from renewed observance of Erkenwald's feast. It must not be exposed as an exact composition date.

date_statements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century (sole witness; connected with a 1477 scribe)

assertion.notes: The BL record dates the codex 1450-1499 and says fols. 65-83 are probably in the hand associated with John Watton's 1477 colophon.
Horstmann's complete text is the earliest edition identified by the checked scholarship.

places.label: Cheshire, England
notes: DIMEV/eLALME Linguistic Profile 419 localizes the language of Saint Erkenwald in the sole witness to Cheshire.

scope.description: Language of the poem in the sole surviving manuscript, not a proved authorial place of composition

assertion.notes: This statement is specific to fols. 72v-75v and eLALME LP 419; other sections of Harley MS 2250 have other profiles.

scope.description: Proposed audience and patronal context in Turville-Petre's 2024 edition, as reported by Battles

assertion.notes: The current edition challenges the conventional 'London poem' label and argues for a Cheshire audience and patronal setting. The reviewed claim does not establish a certain authorial birthplace or exact place of composition.

scope.description: The action centers on St Paul's Cathedral in London, imagined also as ancient New Troy.
Association with the cult of Saint Erkenwald and St Paul's Cathedral

assertion.notes: The strong London association does not prove that the poem was composed in London.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The Latin rubric/title and modern-English U-M framing do not make the substantively Middle English poem multilingual for whole-work filtering.

form_statements.description: A 352-line unrhymed alliterative poem in long lines; CME90053 encodes each line as an L element.

assertion.notes: DIMEV and the BL catalog classify the item as a life of Saint Erkenwald, though the poem narrates one miracle rather than a cradle-to-grave biography.
The resurrected judge functions as an ethical exemplar of impartial justice; 'exemplum' is a secondary generic facet, not a replacement for miracle narrative or hagiography.

subject_statements.label: Conjuration, exorcism, and discernment of the dead

assertion.notes: Gordon identifies Erkenwald's command at lines 179-184 as a formal conjuration and contextualizes it through medieval exorcism, discernment and apotropaic practice.

summaries.value: During rebuilding at St Paul's Cathedral, workers discover an undeciphered marble tomb containing the incorrupt body of a richly dressed man. Bishop Erkenwald summons the corpse to speak. It identifies itself as a just pagan judge of ancient New Troy who was honored for impartial judgment but remained excluded from salvation because he had never received Christian baptism. As Erkenwald utters the Trinitarian baptismal formula, his tears fall on the corpse and become baptismal water; the released soul enters heaven, the body decays, and London responds with procession and bells.

After a modern-English U-M opener and argument, CME90053 supplies three navigation divisions: [Prologue], lines 1-33, on London, conversion and the rebuilding of St Paul's; Fit I, lines 34-176, on discovery of the tomb, the incorrupt body and Erkenwald's arrival; and Fit II, lines 177-352, on the pagan judge's testimony, salvation without baptism, Erkenwald's tears, the soul's release and the closing procession. These are encoding/editorial divisions, not a complete manuscript section hierarchy. Scholarship describes the first 32 lines as the historical introduction and reports manuscript capitals only at lines 1 and 177.

extent.description: One complete 352-line Middle English verse work plus one modern-English U-M editorial-framing unit. The poem has no LG verse-group elements. Its three DIV2 containers ([Prologue] and two fits) are counted as encoding-level navigation sections rather than three separate medieval works or a complete manuscript hierarchy.

content_parts.label: U-M editorial opener and argument
content_parts.summary: A modern-English byline and February 2023 dateline identify the OTA/Gollancz basis, followed by an argument explaining lowercase transcription, thorn conversion, removed punctuation, added folio and line references, and treatment of Gollancz's emendations and manuscript readings.

locators.value: BODY/OPENER and BODY/ARGUMENT before DIV1 TYPE='poem'

notes: This retained encoding-layer paratext is modern English. It does not change the abstract medieval work's Middle English language or verse form.

content_parts.label: U-M navigation: [Prologue]
content_parts.summary: CME90053 groups lines 1-33 under [PROLOGUE]. The passage recalls the conversion of England and London, the rededication of pagan temples, London's identity as New Troy and Erkenwald's episcopate at St Paul's.
notes: The opening CME milestone 72b is equivalent to 72v; a/b and r/v are alternate leaf-side abbreviations.

Gollancz prints a bracketed [PROLOGUE] label, and CME ends its prologue division at line 33. Schustereder instead describes the first 32 lines as the historical introduction and reports no manuscript division at line 33; the navigation label is not cataloged as authorial manuscript structure.

content_parts.label: U-M navigation: Fit I
content_parts.summary: CME90053 groups lines 34-176 as Fit I. Builders find the carved marble tomb and an undecayed crowned body; London crowds to the discovery; Erkenwald returns from Essex, celebrates mass and approaches the unknown corpse after written searches fail.

locators.value: approximately ff. 72v-74r, inferred from CME milestones 72b-74a

notes: The folio span follows the established a=recto and b=verso crosswalk; exact line placement can be visually checked if images become available.
Schustereder reports no manuscript capital or other division at line 34. 'Fit I' is retained as U-M navigation rather than asserted as manuscript structure.

content_parts.label: U-M navigation: Fit II
content_parts.summary: CME90053 groups lines 177-352 as Fit II. The corpse recounts a life of impartial pagan justice and exclusion from Christian salvation; Erkenwald longs to baptize him; a tear falls during the baptismal formula, the soul is released, the body turns to dust and the city processes with its bells ringing.

locators.value: approximately ff. 74r-75v, inferred from CME milestones 74a-75b

notes: Line 177 coincides with the manuscript's second reported major capital, but the 'Fit II' label remains U-M navigation rather than a transcribed manuscript heading. The folio span follows the established a/r and b/v crosswalk and can be visually checked if images become available.
Saint Erkenwald ends on f. 75v, where the following Life of St John the Baptist also begins; this is a shared physical boundary.

related_works.title: Pearl, Patience, Cleanness, and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
notes: Stylistic, metrical and lexical affinities have supported a common-authorship hypothesis, but shared authorship is disputed and these works survive in a different manuscript, dialect and hand.
The relation records a scholarly comparison, not a source-text derivation or confirmed authorial corpus.

evidence.locator: Complete HEADER and TEXT; BODY/OPENER, BODY/ARGUMENT and poem lines 1-352
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for identifiers, titles, the Middle English verse work, modern-English editorial framing, U-M navigation divisions, content, line count, folio milestones, setting, subjects and Gollancz/OTA editorial mediation.

The approved source contains one modern-English OPENER/ARGUMENT framing unit, one poem, three DIV2 navigation sections, 352 numbered L elements and seven folio milestones. The three verse ranges contain 33, 143 and 176 lines respectively.

evidence.citation: Israel Gollancz, ed., St. Erkenwald (London: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1922).
evidence.locator: Preface pp. v-viii and lv-lxii; poem pp. 1-14 / PDF pp. 73-86
evidence.summary: Evidence for title, editor/transcriber, unique witness, editorial quatrains and [PROLOGUE] label, content, alliterative verse, conjectural 1386 date, speculative Ralph Strode attribution, London association, justice theme, related-poem comparisons, exact printed-page locators and the internal [F. 75b] milestone that contradicts the preface's 75a endpoint.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, Altenglische Legenden, Neue Folge (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1881), 265-274.
evidence.summary: Evidence for Horstmann's predecessor edition, editor responsibility, manuscript source, alliterative form and first-known publication date.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2250, record 040-002048081.
evidence.locator: Scope and Content; Date Range; Origin and Provenance
evidence.summary: Evidence for the unique-work location at fols. 72v-75v, its shared final-leaf boundary with the following Life of St John the Baptist, Middle English language, religious manuscript context, second-half-fifteenth-century witness date, Cheshire origin of the relevant scribal block and 1477 hand connection.

evidence.locator: Required information for manuscript orders and recto/verso terminology
evidence.summary: Official evidence that recto is often abbreviated r or a and verso v or b, establishing the side-notation crosswalk used for CME's 72b-75b milestones; also provides the current commissioned-imaging route.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, record 706 and manuscript entry BLHar2250.
evidence.summary: Evidence for DIMEV/NIMEV identifiers, incipit, explicit, hagiographic subject, alliterative unrhymed verse, sole witness and folios, manuscript rubric, edition bibliography and work-specific eLALME LP 419 Cheshire localization.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive item 0053 TEI header.
evidence.summary: Evidence for OTA identifier, title forms, Middle English language, Gollancz source and legacy digital-text layer.

evidence.citation: Stefan Schustereder, 'Coming to Terms with a Pagan Past: The Story of St Erkenwald,' Studia Anglica Posnaniensia 48.2-3 (2013), 71-92, doi:10.2478/stap-2013-0008.
evidence.summary: Peer-reviewed synthesis supporting the unique witness and witness date; Peterson's probably-post-1386/no-later-than-1420 discussion; Northwest Midland language; John Massey of Cotton attribution history; disputed Pearl/Gawain authorship; manuscript capitals at lines 1 and 177 with no further manuscript or strophic divisions; the 32-line historical introduction; London setting; and pagan-past themes.
notes: The ignored local cache is the complete 22-page article.

evidence.citation: Thorlac Turville-Petre, ed., St Erkenwald: A Critical Edition (Liverpool: Liverpool University Press, 2024).
evidence.locator: JSTOR and publisher/distributor records; DIMEV Bibliography.xml key Turville-Petre2024
evidence.summary: Evidence for the current critical edition's editor, title, publication and related-source status. The full edition was not publicly downloadable and is not treated as an inspected primary-transmission intermediary.

evidence.citation: Dominique Battles, review of Thorlac Turville-Petre, St Erkenwald: A Critical Edition, The Medieval Review 25.09.30 (2025).
evidence.locator: Complete review, especially edition contents, Cheshire context and authorship discussion
evidence.summary: Independent review evidence that the 2024 edition contains a facing-page text and translation with apparatus and that it challenges the conventional London-poem label, argues for a Cheshire audience and patronal context, and reopens but does not resolve Pearl-poet common authorship.

evidence.citation: Stephen Gordon, 'Conjuring the Dead in St Erkenwald,' The Review of English Studies 77.330 (2026): 286-299, doi:10.1093/res/hgaf097.
evidence.locator: Complete article; unique witness and composition context on p. 1; lines 179-184 throughout
evidence.summary: Current peer-reviewed evidence for the poem's unique witness and the narrative and theological importance of Erkenwald's conjuration of the corpse, contextualized through medieval exorcism, discernment and apotropaic practice.
notes: The ignored local cache is the complete 611,643-byte accepted PDF, published under CC BY 4.0.

open_questions.question: Can current scholarship narrow the poem's composition date, place or authorship beyond an anonymous late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century alliterative work with Northwest Midland/Cheshire language and a London setting?
next_steps: Consult the complete Turville-Petre 2024 edition beyond its verified record and independent review before adopting its Cheshire audience, patron or author profile as fact.
Keep Gollancz's 1386 date and London-author argument, his Ralph Strode speculation, Peterson's John Massey of Cotton candidate, and Gawain/Pearl Poet association attributed and qualified.
Do not convert work-specific witness dialect into a certain authorial birthplace or composition place.

open_questions.question: Can manuscript images visually confirm the reported capitals at lines 1 and 177 and the absence of further codicological division marks?
next_steps: Verify the two reported capitals and surrounding layout against complete Harley MS 2250 images when available.
Continue to model Gollancz's quatrains and [PROLOGUE] label and U-M's lines 1-33, 34-176 and 177-352 divisions as editorial/encoding navigation.
Preserve the distinction between Schustereder's 32-line historical introduction and U-M's 33-line [Prologue] division.

open_questions.question: Can restored or commissioned images confirm the exact line placement of CME's internal folio milestones against the current BL pencil foliation?
next_steps: Treat a=recto and b=verso as established equivalent notation, following the current BL imaging guidance.
Preserve Gollancz/OTA's 75a endpoint as a source-layer error because Gollancz's poem, CME, BL and DIMEV support 75b/75v.
Use images only to verify exact line-to-folio transitions and the shared f. 75v boundary, not to reopen the a/b crosswalk without contrary evidence.

notes: The abstract medieval poem is distinct from the historical Saint Erkenwald, his full life, Harley MS 2250 as a physical codex, Gollancz's 1922 edition, OTA 0053 and CME90053.

Anonymous authorship is the default. The Gawain/Pearl Poet association, Gollancz's Ralph Strode speculation and Peterson's John Massey of Cotton candidate are retained only as low-confidence attribution history.

The composition statement normalizes only the sourced upper bound of 1420. Schustereder's 'probably slightly later than 1386' report is preserved in prose rather than converted into an invented exact lower year; Gollancz's about-1386 claim is separately modeled as a low-confidence conjecture.
Cheshire/eLALME LP 419 describes the language of the sole surviving witness at fols. 72v-75v. It does not prove an authorial origin or composition place.

Legacy a/b and current r/v are equivalent side notation. The Gollancz/OTA 75a endpoint is retained as a source-layer error because Gollancz's own [F. 75b] marker, CME, BL and DIMEV establish 75b/75v; f. 75v is shared with the following Life of St John the Baptist.
The modern-English U-M opener and argument are modeled as encoding-layer prose paratext; discovery language and form remain Middle English and verse for the abstract medieval work.

Complete public scans of the Gollancz and Horstmann editions, OTA files, DIMEV v1.0 archive, Schustereder and Gordon articles, and the BL price list are cached under source-cache/CME90053. The manuscript images remain uncached because the legacy BL object is unavailable and its published extent cannot be verified.
